summary: Narrative review of MIS techniques for thoracolumbar burst fractures covering construct length, cement augmentation, reduction techniques, and implant removal.
main_conclusion: Percutaneous pedicle screw fixation with index-level screws and subsequent implant removal shows promise for AO Type A3/A4 burst fractures.

summary: Finite element analysis of 6 models comparing 4-screw vs 6-screw posterior short-segment fixation for osteoporotic T12 burst fractures under mechanical loading.
main_conclusion: Six-screw constructs with intermediate screws reduce ROM and axial micromotion, supporting more rigid fixation in osteoporotic burst fractures.

summary: Retrospective study of 51 patients with osteoporotic TLBFs treated by unilateral extrapedicular percutaneous kyphoplasty combined with 6-screw fixation.
main_conclusion: The hybrid technique achieved significant pain relief (VAS/ODI, P<0.05) with 98% midline cement diffusion and 5.8% leakage rate.

summary: Prospective RCT of 150 patients (LSC score ≤6) comparing posterior short-segment fixation with vs without intermediate screws for thoracolumbar burst fractures.
main_conclusion: Both 4-screw and 6-screw fixation showed comparable long-term outcomes; 4-screw had shorter operative time (P=0.001) and less blood loss.

summary: Retrospective analysis of 134 patients finding 47% incidence of fracture-associated vacuum phenomenon (FAVP) at mean 10 months after thoracolumbar burst fractures.
main_conclusion: FAVP was associated with disc injuries, diabetes, Type A3/A4 fractures, male gender, and PLC injury, and correlated with worse pain outcomes.

summary: Retrospective review of 141 elderly patients (≥65y) with upper cervical fractures showing 9.9% incidence of out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with 21.4% survival.
main_conclusion: OHCA after upper cervical fractures presents with non-shockable rhythms (PEA/asystole); Anderson type II odontoid fracture predicted OHCA (P<0.05).

summary: Retrospective analysis of 22,440 type II odontoid fracture patients evaluating frailty impact on outcomes using the 5-factor modified frailty index.
main_conclusion: Operative treatment showed 43-77% mortality reduction vs nonoperative across all frailty strata with dose-dependent frailty association.

summary: Multicenter retrospective study of 20,566 cervical SCI patients from 503 trauma centers evaluating disparities in time to surgical decompression (mean 30h).
main_conclusion: Black race (+1.4h), lack of insurance (+1.6h), and frailty (+3.4h) independently predicted delayed surgery with unexplained institutional variation.

summary: Case report of a 63-year-old male who developed quadriplegia from spinal cord reperfusion injury 4 hours after ACDF, treated with emergency laminoplasty.
main_conclusion: Early recognition and urgent posterior decompression with high-dose corticosteroid therapy achieved full neurological recovery at 2 months.

summary: Case report and literature review of a 32-year-old with cervical penetrating spinal cord injury from an arrow, managed with surgical extraction in Kenya.
main_conclusion: Safe timely arrow removal with prophylactic antibiotics is the primary goal; MAP augmentation is unsupported for penetrating spinal injuries.

summary: Retrospective study of 1,032 patients developing an 8-factor prediction model for 12-month functional recovery after thoracolumbar incomplete SCI (AUC 0.87).
main_conclusion: Admission AIS grade (OR 4.5), decompression ≤24h (OR 1.9), and intact PLC (OR 2.3) were the strongest independent predictors of functional recovery.

summary: Retrospective cohort study of 1,038 SCI patients (median decompression time 25h) evaluating the impact of surgical timing on bladder and sexual function at 1 year.
main_conclusion: Shorter time to decompression was not associated with improved bladder or sexual function; older age and injury severity predicted outcomes.

summary: Case report of a 61-year-old male who developed quadriparesis after posterior cervical decompression with T2 hyperintense MRI signal consistent with white cord syndrome.
main_conclusion: Immediate high-dose methylprednisolone led to partial neurological recovery, emphasizing the importance of early WCS recognition by spine surgeons.

summary: Retrospective comparison of 34 neurologically intact thoracolumbar burst fracture patients: percutaneous pedicle screws alone (n=17) vs with vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty (n=17).
main_conclusion: Anterior support provided superior immediate kyphosis correction, but both groups converged to comparable alignment (7.9° vs 7.73°) by 6 months.

summary: Retrospective comparison of O-arm navigation (n=11) vs C-arm fluoroscopy (n=13) for percutaneous pedicle screw fixation in ankylosing spinal disorder fractures.
main_conclusion: O-arm navigation reduced radiation exposure time significantly (2.5 vs 6.4 min) without compromising screw placement accuracy in ASD fractures.

summary: Narrative review examining whether the cervicothoracic junction (CTJ) should be a modifier in subaxial cervical spine fracture classification systems.
main_conclusion: CTJ fractures exhibit distinct anatomical and biomechanical features supporting consideration as a meaningful classification modifier.

summary: Systematic review of 483 articles distilled to 11 pioneering subaxial cervical spine classification systems, tracing evolution to the AO classification.
main_conclusion: The AO Cervical Spine Injury Classification provides a structured framework with type A/B/C escalation and modifiers to guide management.

summary: Narrative review of floating lateral mass (F3) fractures of the subaxial cervical spine, examining diagnosis, stability assessment, and surgical management.
main_conclusion: Floating lateral mass fractures should be considered potentially unstable; MRI is essential for detecting disco-ligamentous injuries.

summary: Retrospective study of 49 unilateral cervical facet fractures correlating CT characteristics with MRI instability scores to predict need for operative stabilization.
main_conclusion: Fracture displacement (P=0.013) and multifragmentary pattern (P<0.001), not fragment size, correlated with MRI instability and operative necessity.

summary: AO Spine Knowledge Forum review of 4 impactful studies on subaxial cervical fracture management covering imaging, conservative failure predictors, and surgical approach.
main_conclusion: CT/MRI is recommended as gold standard for workup; anterior fixation is conditionally recommended for unilateral facet injuries without cord injury.
